schools.summary: The women buy spices to anoint the body — proof they did not expect the resurrection, which strengthens its evidence (Barnes, Clarke); after the sabbath, early Sunday.
A happy day dawns, the Lord rising in triumph (Pseudo-Jerome); we come to his sepulchre with the spices of virtue and good works (Gregory).
Calvin: Jewish anointing confirmed faith in the resurrection — God's image of life in death; Henry: a pious, not superstitious, visit.

The women came at dawn; 'while it was yet dark' (John) and 'at the rising of the sun' (Mark) harmonized by the gradual dawn (Augustine, Lightfoot's four twilights) — they set out dark, arrived at sunrise; the resurrection 'on the third day' (Robertson).

outliers.note: The Cyril fragment here, on the Church built of the Gentiles, touches the new age but is a broader catechetical aside.

themes: at the rising of the sun

schools.summary: The women's anxious question (peculiar to Mark) shows no hope of resurrection (Robertson); they knew not of the sealed stone and guard (Wesley); the difficulty was already removed (Henry).
Calvin: a holy but unconsidered zeal that God does not charge as fault — fear and astonishment had robbed them of thought; they came without due consideration.

themes: who shall roll away the stone

schools.summary: The women look up and see the great stone already rolled away (Mark's vivid present) — its very greatness (the clause linked to v.3) magnifying the wonder of its removal by the divine hand (Matthew 28:2).
Bede: the rolled-away stone figures the opening of the mysteries once veiled under the law written on stone.
An angel as a young man in white (Mark), 'angel' in Matthew, 'two men' in Luke — variations showing independent witness, strengthening the resurrection evidence (Robertson); the women utterly amazed.
Gregory/Chrysologus: the right-hand seat = everlasting life, the white robe of heavenly light, the young man the 'age of joy' — the resurrection has no old age.
The angel stills their fear and announces the simple fact: 'He is risen; he is not here,' pointing to the empty place as proof; 'the crucified' become his crown of glory (Galatians 6:14, Robertson).
Henry: the Crucified is the Risen One — his shame wipes not the glory but the glory the reproach; the body risen, not stolen.

themes: he is risen, he is not here

schools.summary: 'And Peter' (only in Mark, gratefully remembered by Peter behind this Gospel) — a tender mercy to the penitent denier, assuring him he is still owned and loved by the risen Christ (Barnes, Gill).
Henry: a sight of Christ welcome to the true penitent, and the penitent welcome to Christ; past sins, no longer pleasing, cease to hurt (Pseudo-Jerome).

The women flee trembling, mixed fear and joy too great for speech (reconciled with Matthew's 'great joy,' Augustine); the two oldest MSS (Aleph, B) end here, gravely doubting the genuineness of vv.9-20 (Robertson).
Christ appeared first to Mary Magdalene; but the grammatical voices note vv.9-20 are absent from the oldest MSS (Vaticanus, Sinaiticus), gravely doubted as the longer ending (Robertson, Clarke).

Receiving the verses, the fathers read Mary's priority as grace abounding over sin (Romans 5:20): she who first tasted death's guilt becomes the first herald of life (Theophylact, Bede); the seven devils figure all vices (Gregory).
Henry: the forgiven woman who loved much was honoured as the first to see the risen Christ — the closer we cleave to Christ, the sooner we see him.

sides.position: Gravely doubted — absent from the oldest and best MSS (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus)
Received as Scripture and expounded by the ancient fathers and the mass of MSS

schools.summary: Mary reports to the mourning, weeping disciples (their grief fulfilling John 16:20); the unusual phrase 'them that had been with him' is among the marks suggesting another hand for the longer ending (Robertson).
Henry: no better news for disciples in tears than the resurrection — we should comfort mourners with what we have seen of Christ.
The disciples' disbelief proves they did not expect the resurrection and were not easily deceived (Barnes); their slowness providentially strengthens the credibility of their later testimony (Henry).
Calvin: a culpable, shameful indifference — they held as a dream what the Son of God had so often promised and eye-witnesses confirmed; unbelief robbed them of sound understanding.
The Emmaus appearance 'in another form' — not a changed face but their eyes held from recognizing him (Luke 24:16); told in full by Luke; his countenance glorified, not lost (Chrysologus, Henry).
Pseudo-Jerome: the walking disciples figure the believer's pilgrimage — now seeing Christ through a glass, hereafter face to face.

themes: two on the road to Emmaus

schools.summary: The two from Emmaus report, still met with disbelief; the tension with Luke 24:34 ('risen indeed') resolved as a wavering, conjectural hope, not yet full faith (Lightfoot, Wesley); the gradual proof providential (Henry).
Christ appears to the eleven at meat (cf. Luke 24:36-43; John 20:19), rebuking their unbelief; Augustine harmonizes 'the last time' and Thomas's absence.
The rebuke of love — that faith replace unbelief, a heart of flesh the stony heart (Pseudo-Jerome); the eating proves the reality of the risen flesh (Gregory).
Henry: while Christ comforts his people, he sees it needful to rebuke their hardness in distrusting his promise — a correcting love.

themes: the reality of the risen flesh

schools.summary: The Great Commission (cf. Matthew 28:16-20): preach to all the world, every creature — Gentile as well as Jew, breaking every barrier (Barnes); the universal reversal of 'go not to the Gentiles' (Wesley).
Gregory: 'every creature' = every man, for whose sake all was created; the preaching once thrust from Judaea now goes to all.

themes: go ye into all the world
preach the gospel to every creature

schools.summary: Belief and baptism unto salvation, unbelief unto condemnation; the second clause omits baptism — condemnation rests on unbelief alone (Robertson, Wesley); Barnes stresses baptism's importance though not absolute necessity.
Calvin: baptism is the testimony/sign of salvation joined to faith, not half its cause — not necessary in itself but with respect to obedience; faith the decisive thing.

sides.position: No — condemnation rests on unbelief alone (baptism omitted in the second clause); baptism is the sign, not the cause
Strongly important though not absolutely necessary — its neglect endangers the soul

themes: he that believeth and is baptized
he that believeth not shall be damned
baptism the sign not the cause

schools.summary: The signs (casting out demons, new tongues — fulfilled at Pentecost) wrought 'in Christ's name,' not the apostles' own (Barnes); necessary to establish religion, not for all believers nor now (Barnes, Gill).
Calvin: miracles promised only for a time, to give lustre to the new gospel, then ceasing — later forged miracles opened the door to Satan's impostures (Matthew 24:24).
Gregory: the Church daily does in spirit what the apostles did in body — casting out devils, speaking a new language by the holy mysteries.

debates.question: Are the miraculous signs temporary or perpetual?

sides.position: Temporary — to confirm the new gospel, then ceasing
An ongoing spiritual fulfilment in the Church / latent power in faith

schools.summary: Serpent-immunity (Paul at Malta, Acts 28:5) and healing the sick (Acts) authenticated the apostolic mission; no warrant for presumptuous testing (Wesley); grave doubt over these verses' genuineness urges caution (Robertson).

The ascension (located by Luke on Olivet after forty days) and session at God's right hand — the highest honour and sovereignty (Barnes; Acts 7:55; Hebrews 1:3); the author passes into theology (Swete).
Christ ascends by his own power, unlike Elijah's chariot (Gregory); the session signifies rule and the coming judgeship; the 'right hand' is power received as man, not bodily limbs (Augustine).
Henry: sitting is rest (his work finished) and rule (his kingdom possessed) — glorified with the glory he had before the world.

themes: sat on the right hand of God

schools.summary: The apostles preach everywhere, the Lord working with them and confirming the word by signs (Barnes); the cooperation internal and external, still needful for successful preaching though miracles have ceased (Clarke); 'Amen' likely a later addition.
Henry: the apostles labour, God works effectually; the signs that follow — the world's reformation, idolatry's fall, sinners converted — are the gospel's standing proof.
